key_insight: Elastic surface wrinkling in a compressed hyperelastic half-space is reinterpreted as a topological phase transition, where increasing compression tunes a Dirac mass and changes the winding number.
engineering_relevance: This provides a new physics-based framework for predicting and potentially programming surface instabilities in soft materials and smart structures.
method: Generalized Stroh formalism with Lie group metric evolution to model nonlinear geometric frustration.
Map the surface impedance matrix to an effective 1D Dirac Hamiltonian.
Track topology via winding number and the emergence of a zero-energy edge state at wrinkle onset.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an analytical mapping and theoretical demonstration of a quantized winding-number jump at the onset of surface wrinkles, but no numerical dataset or experimental validation is described.
watch_item: The result is theoretically elegant, but practical use will depend on how well the idealized half-space, finite-compression assumptions match real materials and boundary conditions.
paper_title: Elastic Surface Instability as a Topological Phase Transition
